model_narrative: We plan a surveillance flight for an aircraft that must scan a collection of swaths, where each swath is a region scanned by flying through it between one of its valid pairs of entry and exit nodes. The aircraft flies a single tour that enters and leaves each swath once, and we decide which arcs to fly between swath node combinations and the resulting order in which the swaths are visited. Each arc has a known flight distance. The objective is to minimize the total flight distance of the tour.

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the swaths, which are the regions that must be scanned; the first swath serves as the start and end region of the tour and the remaining swaths are the regions to be visited

sets.name: n
sets.doc: the candidate entry and exit nodes of a swath; each swath is scanned by traversing it between one of its valid node combinations

sets.name: a
sets.members: the valid arcs, listed as four-tuples (from-swath, from-node, to-swath, to-node)
sets.doc: the set of valid arcs that can be flown; each arc connects a node combination of one swath to a node combination of a different swath, so an arc is given as a from-swath, a from-node, a to-swath, and a to-node, and no arc stays within the same swath

sets.name: sx
sets.members: the valid (swath, node) combinations that appear as the origin endpoint of some arc
sets.doc: the valid swath-node combinations, meaning the (swath, node) pairs that are actually usable as endpoints of flown arcs; a (swath, node) pair not in this set is never a valid place to enter or leave

params.name: l
params.doc: the flight distance of each arc, that is the length flown when traveling from the arc's origin swath-node combination to its destination swath-node combination, in distance units

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the arc-use indicator; equals 1 if that arc is flown as part of the tour and 0 otherwise

vars.name: y
vars.doc: the swath-ordering indicator over an ordered pair of swaths; equals 1 if the second swath is visited immediately after the first swath in the tour and 0 otherwise; only meaningful for pairs of distinct swaths

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the total length of the chosen tour, in distance units

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Every swath must be entered exactly once by the tour. For each swath, count the flown arcs whose destination lies in that swath across all of its node combinations, and require that this count equals one.

constraints.name: sx:
constraints.intent: The flight must be continuous at every usable swath-node combination, so the aircraft leaves a combination exactly when it arrives there. For each valid swath-node combination, the number of flown arcs arriving at that combination must equal the number of flown arcs departing from it. Combinations that are not valid endpoints impose no such requirement.

constraints.name: y[i,
constraints.intent: The order in which swaths are visited follows from the arcs actually flown between them. For each ordered pair of distinct swaths, the indicator that the second swath is visited immediately after the first must equal the total number of flown arcs that go from any node combination of the first swath to any node combination of the second.

constraints.name: z
constraints.intent: The total tour length accumulates the distance of every flown arc. Set the total tour length equal to the sum over all arcs of each arc's flight distance counted only when that arc is flown.

query: We're curious how tied the plan is to bouncing straight back and forth between the swaths in the opening stretch of the map. Suppose that, anywhere among the earliest third of the swaths, we don't let the aircraft fly both directions of the same direct hop — at most one of each such back-and-forth pair. What total distance does the optimizer return?
expected_pyomo:
def _r(model, i, j): return model.y[i, j] + model.y[j, i] <= 1 if (int(i[1:]) < int(j[1:]) and int(j[1:]) < len(model.s) / 3.0) else Constraint.Skip
model.no2cyc_s2s4 = Constraint(model.s, model.s, rule=_r)

query: Let's widen that out to the earlier half of the route — what does the plan cost if, among that first half of the swaths, we rule out the aircraft shuttling directly both ways on any single hop, keeping at most one direction of each such pair?
expected_pyomo:
def _r(model, i, j): return model.y[i, j] + model.y[j, i] <= 1 if (int(i[1:]) < int(j[1:]) and int(j[1:]) < len(model.s) / 2.0) else Constraint.Skip
model.no2cyc_s5s7 = Constraint(model.s, model.s, rule=_r)

query: There's a proposal on the table to keep swath s2 from being a routing afterthought relative to s1 — the idea is that the surveillance order should hand off into s2 at least as often as it hands off into s1, counting the inbound legs from the other near-cluster swaths around them. If we tie the inbound traffic into s2 to be no less than the inbound traffic into s1, what total flight distance does the optimizer come back with?
expected_pyomo: model.couple_in_s2_ge_s1 = Constraint(expr=model.y['s0', 's2'] + model.y['s1', 's2'] + model.y['s3', 's2'] >= model.y['s0', 's1'] + model.y['s2', 's1'] + model.y['s3', 's1'])

query: We're looking at how much the mission really needs to dwell inside the opening near cluster — the four swaths s0, s1, s2, s3 that sit close together at the start of the map. The thought is to allow at most one direct hop that stays within that little group, so the aircraft is pushed to break out toward the rest of the swaths instead of circling among those four. If we cap the within-cluster legs to a single hop, what total flight distance does the optimizer return?
expected_pyomo: model.cap_near_cluster_hops = Constraint(expr=sum(model.y[i, j] for i in ['s0', 's1', 's2', 's3'] for j in ['s0', 's1', 's2', 's3'] if i != j) <= 1)

query: I'm looking at this plan and right through the middle stretch of the route the aircraft just ping-pongs — out to a swath and straight back. Those aren't real surveillance tours, they're little loops. Shouldn't we forbid taking both directions of any single hop among that middle band of swaths so the route has to connect somewhere else? What is the model seeing that makes those little loops attractive?
expected_pyomo:
def _r(model, i, j): return model.y[i, j] + model.y[j, i] <= 1 if (int(i[1:]) < int(j[1:]) and len(model.s) / 4.0 <= int(i[1:]) and int(j[1:]) < 3 * len(model.s) / 4.0) else Constraint.Skip
model.no_loop_s6s8 = Constraint(model.s, model.s, rule=_r)

query: Honestly the same complaint applies all over the map — the route keeps closing tiny two-swath loops instead of threading through the area. Couldn't we just ban flying both directions of any hop, anywhere in the plan, so every swath has to link onward to a new one? Why is the optimizer happy parking the aircraft in all these loops?

model.no_loop_s9s11 = Constraint(model.s, model.s, rule=_r)

query: The tail end of the route is yet another stretch full of these closed two-swath shuttles. It can't be right that the best mission plan parks the aircraft in isolated back-and-forths out in the later swaths. Shouldn't we at least stop it from flying both directions of any hop among that later half of the map? What's the tradeoff the model is making by keeping those loops?
expected_pyomo:
def _r(model, i, j): return model.y[i, j] + model.y[j, i] <= 1 if (int(i[1:]) < int(j[1:]) and int(i[1:]) >= len(model.s) / 2.0) else Constraint.Skip
model.no_loop_s18s20 = Constraint(model.s, model.s, rule=_r)

constraints.intent: The order in which swaths are visited follows from the arcs actually flown between them. For each ordered pair of distinct swaths, the indicator that the second swath is visited immediately after the first must equal the total number of flown arcs that go from any node combination of the first swath to any node combination of the second.

constraints.name: z
constraints.intent: The total tour length accumulates the distance of every flown arc. Set the total tour length equal to the sum over all arcs of each arc's flight distance counted only when that arc is flown.

query: Looking at the plan, the four opening swaths s0, s1, s2, s3 just feed into each other — the aircraft keeps threading from one of those near-cluster swaths straight into another one instead of striking out toward the rest of the map. That feels like wasted time penned up in that corner. Shouldn't each of those four swaths hand off directly to a swath outside the cluster rather than to one of its three neighbors? What's the model seeing that makes it keep those internal hand-offs?
